content: Just got back from a camping trip to Yellowstone with my brother. We saw a grizzly bear from about 50 yards away - absolutely terrifying but incredible. My hands were shaking so bad I could barely take a photo.
Wow, that must have been both scary and amazing! Was this your first time seeing a grizzly in the wild?

Yeah, first time. We've been hiking in national parks for years but never saw anything bigger than a moose. My brother's been talking about getting proper bear spray ever since. He's the more cautious one - I'm the one who wanted to hike off-trail to find that hidden hot spring we read about online.
Sounds like you balance each other out well on these trips. Did you find the hot spring?

We did! It was this perfect little pool with a view of the valley. Completely worth the extra 3 miles of bushwhacking. Though I did slip on some moss and almost dropped my camera - the one I just bought for this trip, a Sony A7 IV. Been saving for months for it.
That's quite an investment in photography! Are you getting into it as a serious hobby?

Yeah, I've been doing more and more of it. Actually, I'm thinking about doing a photography workshop in Iceland next summer. A friend from my chess club went last year and his photos were insane - these massive waterfalls and black sand beaches. But first I need to pay off the camera...
Iceland would be incredible for photography. Have you been there before?

Never been to Iceland, but I did spend two weeks in Norway a few years back. Did the Lofoten Islands - those mountains coming straight out of the ocean were unreal. That trip is actually what got me interested in hiking more seriously. Before that I was mostly doing urban stuff in Chicago, where I've lived for the past 5 years since leaving the Bay Area.
What prompted the move from the Bay Area to Chicago?

Started my own electrical business and realized I could work remotely for most of it. Chicago's got a great maker scene - I've been getting really into 3D printing and have a little workshop set up in my garage. Built this custom enclosure for my printer that's been getting some attention in the local maker community.
That's impressive! Do you use the 3D printer for your electrical work too?

Sometimes, yeah. Made some custom brackets and cable management stuff that's been super useful. Actually, I'm working on a project right now for a client who wants a custom aquarium lighting system. They have this massive reef tank - 300 gallons - and want programmable LED strips. Been testing different configurations in my own 55-gallon tank at home.
That sounds like a complex project! Do you keep fish yourself?

Yeah, got a little community tank going - tetras, corydoras, and a betta. It's surprisingly relaxing to watch after a day of wiring and circuit boards. Speaking of which, I need to get back to designing this controller. The client wants it done before I leave for a beekeeping conference in Michigan next month. Been keeping bees for about two years now - started with just two hives in my backyard and now I'm up to six.
Beekeeping sounds fascinating! How did you get into that?

Honestly? I was looking for a hobby that got me outside more. Started with a local beekeeping class - cost me about $200 for the basics. Now I'm making my own honey and even experimenting with brewing a honey ale. Though I should probably focus on that Yellowstone photo album I've been putting off editing...
Sounds like you have your hands full with all these amazing projects! The honey ale brewing sounds particularly interesting - is that related to your home brewing hobby?

Yeah, I've been brewing beer for about 3 years now. Started with basic kits, now I'm doing all-grain batches. My IPA recipe won second place at a local competition last year - still pretty proud of that. Anyway, I should probably get back to this controller design. Thanks for listening to me ramble about my various obsessions!
